chunks.page_summary: This page explains how Rheonics' SRD sensor measures and outputs real-time kinematic viscosity, which is derived from simultaneous dynamic viscosity and density measurements.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863
Real-Time Kinematic Viscosity from SRD
Modified on: Tue, 13 Feb, 2024 at 10:50 PM

The kinematic viscosity of a fluid is calculated as the dynamic viscosity divided by the density at the same temperature.

[Image of SME-TRD with SRD probe]
CE
ATEX
Figure 1. SME-TRD with SRD probe

1. How is the kinematic viscosity measured by Rheonics SRD?
The Rheonics SRD measures viscosity and density using a balanced torsional resonator, one end of which is immersed in the fluid of interest. This resonator vibrates torsionally at a given resonant frequency affected by the fluid's density and has a damping as a result of the fluid's viscosity. The sensor electronics calculate the dynamic viscosity and density based on these measurements.

The SRD outputs the kinematic viscosity dividing the dynamic viscosity by the fluid's measured density. It does not require any external input or customer' action to enable the readings.

2. Kinematic viscosity output from SRD
The kinematic viscosity is given as parameter 3 of the sensor's output readings. Review the complete output's parameters here (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000393235-parameter-list-access-for-field-devices).

Through RCP Software
Rheonics Control Panel software (RCP) is used, but not limited, to monitor, configure and log measurements from the SRD sensor in a PC through Ethernet or USB connection.
key_facts: Kinematic viscosity of a fluid is calculated by dividing its dynamic viscosity by its density at the same temperature.
The Rheonics SRD sensor measures viscosity and density using a balanced torsional resonator immersed in the fluid.
The resonator's torsional vibrations at a given resonant frequency are affected by the fluid's density, and damping occurs due to the fluid's viscosity.
The sensor's electronics calculate dynamic viscosity and density from these measurements.
The SRD sensor directly outputs kinematic viscosity by dividing the dynamic viscosity by the measured fluid density.
The SRD sensor does not require external input or customer action to provide readings.
Kinematic viscosity is provided as parameter 3 in the SRD sensor's output readings.
Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) software can monitor, configure, and log measurements from the SRD sensor on a PC via Ethernet or USB.
warnings_notes: ATEX (Compliance mark for equipment intended for use in potentially explosive atmospheres)

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for configuring and displaying kinematic viscosity measurements using the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) software, and briefly covers enabling it via Fieldbus protocols and the SME-TRD display.
chunks.extracted_text:
To plot kinematic viscosity on the RCP Measurement tab, do as follows:
1. Use the Settings Tab, Communication box to connect the sensor to the RCP.
2. Select the Kin. Visc. parameter in the Graph Settings.
3. The RCP uses cSt (centiStoke) units for Kinematic Viscosity.

Figure 2. Enable Kinematic Viscosity in RCP

4. Go to Measurement Tab. Check the Kinematic Viscosity is being displayed (Kin. Visc.).
5. Make sure the sensor led status is green showing Running and E00 (no errors).

Figure 3. Kinematic Viscosity plots in RCP

Through Fieldbus communication protocols
Fieldbus protocols in the sensor are ordered by user upon request. To get the kinematic viscosity through any protocol (i.e. Profinet, Ethernet/IP, HART, etc.) review the manuals for each protocol here.

Through SME-TRD display
To enable the kinematic viscosity value in the SME-TRD display follow next steps.
1. Go to Communication Tab and select Kinematic Visc. from the parameter list. This can be added in any of the three lines.

key_facts: The Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) software is used to connect, configure, and display sensor data, specifically kinematic viscosity.
The RCP uses cSt (centiStoke) units for Kinematic Viscosity measurements and plots.
Sensor connection status and operational status (Running, E00 for no errors) should be verified through the RCP interface.
Fieldbus communication protocols (e.g., Profinet, Ethernet/IP, HART) for Rheonics sensors are available upon user request and require separate manuals for detailed configuration.
Kinematic viscosity values can also be enabled and displayed on the SME-TRD display through its Communication Tab.

The RCP allows for detailed graph settings including auto/fixed ranges for viscosity, density, temperature, and pressure, as well as selection of graph interval and the option to plot damping (df) and resonant frequency (Fres).
diagram_labels: Log and display data with error code
Plot df (damping) and Fres (resonant frequency) instead of Viscosity & Density

Figure 4. Enable Kinematic Viscosity in SME-TRD display

Units to be displayed in the SME-TRD display should be defined prior to order. Contact Rheonics Support Team if change in units is required.

3. What Units are available as output for kinematic viscosity from Rheonics SRD?

Below is the list of units mostly used for the kinematic viscosity of fluids. All are available with Rheonics SRD sensor and can be specified upon sensor order.

m2/s:
Description: This is the SI (International System of Units) standard unit for kinematic viscosity.
Importance: This is the basic unit for kinematic viscosity (length squared divided by time) obtained from dividing the dynamic viscosity [Pa. s = 1 kg/m·s] and the density of a fluid [kg/m3]. It is the main unit used in research and development, scientific publications, and specialized technical applications.

St - Stoke:
Description: Unit from CGS (centimetre–gram–second system of units) system. This unit is equal to 10⁻⁴ m²/s.
Importance: Named after George Stokes due to his research and job related to fluid’s mechanics and viscosity. Less common unit but can be found in older scientific publications and specialized applications.

cSt - CentiStoke:
Description: Reduced unit from a Stoke. This is equal to 10⁻² Stokes or 10⁻⁶ m²/s
Importance: Commonly used in lubricants, automotive, oil and gas industries.
key_facts: The Rheonics Control Panel version is 5.4.0.6.
Kinematic Viscosity can be enabled for display in the SME-TRD interface.
Units for the SME-TRD display must be defined before placing an order.
Rheonics Support Team should be contacted for any required unit changes.
Rheonics SRD sensors offer various units for kinematic viscosity output, which can be specified upon sensor order.
The m²/s is the SI standard unit for kinematic viscosity, derived from dynamic viscosity and fluid density, and is the primary unit in scientific applications.
The Stoke (St) is a CGS unit for kinematic viscosity, equivalent to 10⁻⁴ m²/s, and is less common but found in older publications.
The CentiStoke (cSt) is a unit derived from Stoke, equal to 10⁻² Stokes or 10⁻⁶ m²/s, and is widely used in lubricants, automotive, oil, and gas industries.
diagram_labels: Figure 4. Enable Kinematic Viscosity in SME-TRD display
warnings_notes: Units to be displayed in the SME-TRD display should be defined prior to order. Contact Rheonics Support Team if change in units is required.

chunks.page_summary: This page explains the importance of measuring kinematic viscosity across various industrial applications, detailing its impact on system performance, efficiency, and operating costs, and provides references to related Rheonics products and resources.
chunks.extracted_text:
4. Why is it important to measure kinematic viscosity?
Kinematic viscosity indicates the fluid's internal resistance to flow under its gravity.
It is used mostly in, but not limited to, automotive, chemical, oil and gas industries. The kinematic viscosity is of relevance since it directly influences the automation quality. It can be related to the lubrication capabilities of a fluid, lubrician degradation, fuel efficiency, heat transfer rate in heat exchangers, etc.
Kinematic viscosity is also relevant for pumping systems selection, designer should know the kinematic viscosity at the min and max temperature conditions. High viscosity fluids will reduce the performance of pumps and increase the power requirements.
Kinematic viscosity is also relevant for pumping systems selection, designer should know the kinematic viscosity of the fluid to design the pump for highest efficiency. During operation, pumping characteristics can be adapted using real-time kinematic viscosity from the SRD to achieve highest efficiency. This allows pumps to be adaptable to changing fluids. Given energy consumption for pumps is the largest cost factor for pumping, operating the pumps at maximum efficiency cuts operating costs significantly.

References:
[1]: Viscosity - Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viscosity)
[2]: rheonics SRD Density Meter inline (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/)
[3]: Calibration verification procedure for inline viscometer and density meter (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000390161-calibrating-srv-in-field-verification-and-re-calibration)
[4]: Real-time engine oil condition monitoring » rheonics :: viscometer and density meter (https://rheonics.com/solutions-item/real-time-engine-oil-condition-monitoring/)
key_facts: Kinematic viscosity indicates a fluid's internal resistance to flow under its gravity.
It is primarily used in automotive, chemical, oil, and gas industries.
Kinematic viscosity directly influences automation quality and is related to lubrication capabilities, lubricant degradation, fuel efficiency, and heat transfer.
Knowing kinematic viscosity at minimum and maximum temperature conditions is crucial for selecting pumping systems.
High viscosity fluids can reduce pump performance and increase power requirements.
Designers should know the fluid's kinematic viscosity to design pumps for highest efficiency.
Pumping characteristics can be adapted using real-time kinematic viscosity, for example from an SRD, to achieve optimal efficiency.
Operating pumps at maximum efficiency significantly cuts operating costs, as energy consumption is a major cost factor for pumping.
